pages.authorityGuidePages.05NoContractio: 0/5 (no contraction) to 5/5 (full strength against resistance) bilaterally
pages.authorityGuidePages.2226MeqlMetabo: 22 - 26 mEq/L (metabolic component — controlled by the kidneys)
pages.authorityGuidePages.300KetonesAbgM: 300), ketones, ABG (metabolic acidosis), Kussmaul breathing, fruity breath, dehydration status
pages.authorityGuidePages.30MlhrBeforeAd: 30 mL/hr) before administering magnesium sulfate. Loss of DTRs is the earliest sign of magnesium toxicity. The antidote is calcium gluconate — it must be at the bedside.
pages.authorityGuidePages.3545MmhgRespir: 35 - 45 mmHg (respiratory component — controlled by the lungs)
pages.authorityGuidePages.365375c977995f: 36.5-37.5°C (97.7-99.5°F) oral. Tympanic and temporal are slightly higher than oral. Rectal is 0.5-1°F higher. Axillary is 0.5-1°F lower.
pages.authorityGuidePages.3SalineInfusio: 3% saline: infusion pump, frequent Na⁺ monitoring, correct slowly
pages.authorityGuidePages.80100MmhgOxyge: 80 - 100 mmHg (oxygenation — not directly related to acid-base but critical for assessment)
pages.authorityGuidePages.aSystematicHea: A systematic head-to-toe assessment ensures no body system is missed. While the order may vary between institutions, the principle of systematic evaluation is consistent. On exam questions, you need to know which assessments are relevant for each body system and what abnormal findings indicate.
pages.authorityGuidePages.abcsFirstThenV: ABCs first, then vital signs, pain, surgical site (drainage, bleeding), I&O, bowel sounds, mobility
pages.authorityGuidePages.acetazolamideD: Acetazolamide (Diamox) — a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that causes renal HCO₃⁻ wasting (rarely used)
pages.authorityGuidePages.activeAndPassi: Active and passive range of motion, crepitus, swelling
pages.authorityGuidePages.administerHypo: Administer hypotonic fluids (0.45% NS or D5W) as ordered — correct gradually to prevent cerebral edema
pages.authorityGuidePages.administerIvCa: Administer IV calcium gluconate slowly (10-20 minutes) — monitor for bradycardia and hypotension
pages.authorityGuidePages.afterAddressin: After addressing ABCs, prioritize physiological needs (oxygenation, nutrition, elimination, pain) before safety, then psychosocial, then self-esteem, then self-actualization. Exam trap: a patient
pages.authorityGuidePages.afterThisInter: — "After this intervention, the nurse should assess for..." or "Which finding indicates the treatment is effective?"
pages.authorityGuidePages.albuterolNebul: Albuterol nebulizer — shifts K⁺ into cells via beta-2 receptor stimulation
pages.authorityGuidePages.alertVerbalPai: Alert, Verbal, Pain, Unresponsive (AVPU) or Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS: 3-15)
pages.authorityGuidePages.allLungFieldsA: All lung fields anteriorly and posteriorly — normal: vesicular, bronchial, bronchovesicular
pages.authorityGuidePages.alwaysAssessBe: Always assess before intervening. On exam questions, "assess" or "evaluate" is almost always the correct first action unless the patient is in immediate danger (e.g., airway obstruction, cardiac arrest, hemorrhage). Even then, your assessment is rapid — it just happens in seconds rather than minutes.
pages.authorityGuidePages.anionGapNaClHc: Anion Gap = Na⁺ - (Cl⁻ + HCO₃⁻) — Normal: 8-12 mEq/L
pages.authorityGuidePages.antidoteCalciu: Antidote: Calcium gluconate IV — keep at bedside during magnesium sulfate infusions
pages.authorityGuidePages.anuriaGrossHem: Anuria, gross hematuria, suprapubic distension, flank pain with fever
pages.authorityGuidePages.anxietypanicAt: Anxiety/panic attacks (most common), pain, fever, early sepsis
pages.authorityGuidePages.anxietyrelated: Anxiety-related: calm reassurance, coached slow breathing, paper bag rebreathing (increases CO₂ inhalation) — use cautiously
pages.authorityGuidePages.arterialBloodG: Arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis provides a snapshot of a patient
pages.authorityGuidePages.assessAt45Dist: Assess at 45° — distension above 3 cm suggests right-sided heart failure or fluid overload
pages.authorityGuidePages.assessForDigox: Assess for digoxin toxicity — hypokalemia increases sensitivity to digoxin
pages.authorityGuidePages.assessmentDiag: Assessment → Diagnosis → Planning → Implementation → Evaluation. On exam questions, if you have not assessed the situation yet, assessment is almost always the correct answer. "Assess first, then intervene" — unless there is an immediate life threat.
pages.authorityGuidePages.assessmentIsOn: Assessment is only valuable if it leads to appropriate clinical decisions. Nursing exams test your ability to connect assessment findings to nursing actions using established prioritization frameworks.
pages.authorityGuidePages.assessmentQues: Assessment questions on nursing exams typically follow one of four patterns:
pages.authorityGuidePages.asymmetricWeak: Asymmetric weakness, compartment syndrome signs, new onset of unilateral swelling (DVT)
pages.authorityGuidePages.basedOnTheseAs: — "Based on these assessment findings, the nurse suspects..." or "Which assessment finding is expected for this condition?"
pages.authorityGuidePages.bicarbonateIsB: Bicarbonate is being lost from the body:
pages.authorityGuidePages.breathSoundsCr: Breath sounds (crackles, bronchial), sputum (amount, color, consistency), temperature, SpO₂, WBC count
pages.authorityGuidePages.bronchodilator: Bronchodilators (albuterol) for bronchospasm, suction as needed for secretion clearance
pages.authorityGuidePages.calcitoninBisp: Calcitonin, bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) for malignancy-related hypercalcemia
pages.authorityGuidePages.calciumGlucona: Calcium gluconate IV — stabilizes cardiac membrane (does NOT lower K⁺, protects the heart)
Calcium gluconate: slow IV push (10-20 min), monitor for bradycardia
pages.authorityGuidePages.calciumIsEssen: Calcium is essential for bone integrity, muscle contraction, blood clotting, and nerve transmission. Ionized (free) calcium is the physiologically active form. Always check albumin levels — for every 1 g/dL decrease in albumin, total calcium decreases by approximately 0.8 mg/dL (corrected calcium calculation).
pages.authorityGuidePages.cardiacMonitor: Cardiac monitoring + calcium gluconate (stabilize the heart first)
pages.authorityGuidePages.cardiacTampona: (cardiac tamponade): Hypotension + JVD + Muffled heart sounds → pericardiocentesis needed
pages.authorityGuidePages.checkMagnesium: Check magnesium level — hypokalemia will not correct until hypomagnesemia is treated
pages.authorityGuidePages.chestPainIrreg: Chest pain, irregular rhythm, absent pulses, cool/mottled extremities, JVD
pages.authorityGuidePages.chronicAlcohol: Chronic alcoholism (most common), malnutrition, loop/thiazide diuretics, GI losses, proton pump inhibitors
pages.authorityGuidePages.clearPaleYello: Clear, pale yellow to amber. Dark/concentrated = dehydration. Cloudy/foul-smelling = infection. Pink/red = hematuria
pages.authorityGuidePages.clinicalAssess: Clinical assessment questions comprise approximately 15-25% of nursing licensing exams (NCLEX and REx-PN). These questions test your ability to gather and interpret patient data, recognize abnormal findings, and prioritize nursing actions based on assessment results. The NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model begins with "Recognize Cues" — which is fundamentally assessment.
pages.authorityGuidePages.cnsDisordersHe: CNS disorders (head injury, brain tumor), salicylate (aspirin) toxicity (early)
pages.authorityGuidePages.coDecreases12M: CO₂ decreases 1.2 mmHg for every 1 mEq/L decrease in HCO₃⁻ (Winter
pages.authorityGuidePages.coIncreases07M: CO₂ increases 0.7 mmHg for every 1 mEq/L increase in HCO₃⁻
pages.authorityGuidePages.coRetentionDue: CO₂ retention due to hypoventilation or impaired gas exchange. CO₂ combines with water to form carbonic acid, lowering pH.
pages.authorityGuidePages.colorTemperatu: Color, temperature, moisture, turgor (test over sternum or forehead in elderly), integrity
pages.authorityGuidePages.confusionLetha: Confusion, lethargy, headache, nausea/vomiting, seizures (severe), cerebral edema. Symptoms correlate with rate of decline — acute onset is more dangerous than chronic.
pages.authorityGuidePages.confusionSeizu: (confusion, seizures) → think sodium or severe calcium imbalance
pages.authorityGuidePages.constipationOr: (constipation or diarrhea) → think potassium or calcium
pages.authorityGuidePages.copdLowflowO12: COPD: low-flow O₂ (1-2 L/min, target SpO₂ 88-92%). Do NOT give high-flow O₂ — removes hypoxic respiratory drive
pages.authorityGuidePages.copdMostCommon: COPD (most common), obesity hypoventilation syndrome, kyphoscoliosis, chronic neuromuscular disorders
pages.authorityGuidePages.copdPatientsAr: COPD patients are chronic CO₂ retainers. Their respiratory drive depends on hypoxia, not CO₂ levels. Giving high-flow O₂ eliminates the hypoxic drive and can cause respiratory arrest. Always use low-flow O₂ targeting SpO₂ 88-92%.
pages.authorityGuidePages.correctSlowlyN: Correct slowly: no more than 8-12 mEq/L per 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS)
pages.authorityGuidePages.cracklesFluida: Crackles (fluid/atelectasis), wheezes (bronchospasm), stridor (upper airway), pleural friction rub
pages.authorityGuidePages.d5wIsIsotonicI: D5W is isotonic in the bag but becomes hypotonic once infused (the dextrose is rapidly metabolized, leaving free water). Therefore, D5W should NOT be used for patients with increased ICP or those at risk for cerebral edema. This distinction is commonly tested.
pages.authorityGuidePages.dailyWeightSam: Daily weight (same time, same scale, same clothing), I&O, lung sounds (crackles), JVD, pedal edema, activity tolerance
pages.authorityGuidePages.dataActionResp: Data, Action, Response — focused charting format
pages.authorityGuidePages.decreasedDtrsE: Decreased DTRs (earliest sign), lethargy, bradycardia, respiratory depression, cardiac arrest
pages.authorityGuidePages.diabetesInsipi: Diabetes insipidus, dehydration, excessive sodium intake, hypertonic tube feedings, heatstroke, watery diarrhea without fluid replacement
pages.authorityGuidePages.dialysisDefini: Dialysis — definitive treatment for severe or refractory hyperkalemia
pages.authorityGuidePages.dialysisForSev: Dialysis for severe or refractory elevation
pages.authorityGuidePages.diarrheaMostCo: Diarrhea (most common — GI tract loses HCO₃⁻)
pages.authorityGuidePages.dietarySources: Dietary sources: green leafy vegetables, nuts, legumes, whole grains
pages.authorityGuidePages.diluteIvPotass: Dilute IV potassium: maximum concentration 40 mEq/L for peripheral IV, 80 mEq/L for central line
pages.authorityGuidePages.discontinueAll: Discontinue all potassium supplements and potassium-sparing medications
pages.authorityGuidePages.diureticUseLoo: Diuretic use (loop/thiazide — causes contraction alkalosis via volume depletion)
pages.authorityGuidePages.doNotConfuseDi: Do not confuse dilutional hyponatremia (fluid overload, treat with fluid restriction) with depletional hyponatremia (sodium loss, treat with saline replacement). The treatment is opposite — getting this wrong on the exam means choosing a harmful intervention.
pages.authorityGuidePages.doNotSuppressK: Do NOT suppress Kussmaul respirations — they are the body
pages.authorityGuidePages.documentAsClos: Document as close to the time of assessment as possible. Late entries should be clearly identified
pages.authorityGuidePages.documentWhatYo: Document what you see, hear, measure, and observe — not your interpretation or judgment. Write "patient states
pages.authorityGuidePages.documentationI: Documentation is a legal record of nursing care and a competency tested on both the NCLEX and REx-PN. Proper documentation of assessment findings protects patients, supports continuity of care, and provides legal defense. Exam questions may ask you to identify correct documentation practices or recognize documentation errors.
pages.authorityGuidePages.drainageAmount: Drainage amount/color, tidaling in water seal, subcutaneous emphysema, breath sounds bilateral, tube site integrity
pages.authorityGuidePages.ecgChangesDysr: (ECG changes, dysrhythmias) → think potassium or calcium
pages.authorityGuidePages.eitherExcessiv: Either excessive loss of acid (H⁺) or excessive retention/administration of base (HCO₃⁻).
pages.authorityGuidePages.eitherTooMuchA: Either too much acid production/ingestion or too much bicarbonate loss. Use the anion gap to differentiate.
pages.authorityGuidePages.electrolyteImb: Electrolyte imbalances rarely occur in isolation. Hypomagnesemia often accompanies hypokalemia. Hypocalcemia frequently coexists with hypomagnesemia. When you see one imbalance on an exam question, look for others that commonly co-occur — this pattern recognition is exactly what the NCLEX tests.
pages.authorityGuidePages.electrolyteQue: Electrolyte questions appear on every nursing licensing exam — NCLEX-RN, NCLEX-PN, and REx-PN. They test your ability to recognize imbalances from clinical data, prioritize nursing interventions, and understand the pathophysiology behind treatment decisions. The NCSBN test plan places electrolytes primarily under Physiological Adaptation and Reduction of Risk Potential, which together account for 20-32% of the NCLEX-RN exam.
Electrolyte questions on the NCLEX and REx-PN follow predictable patterns. Knowing these patterns helps you answer efficiently and accurately, even when the clinical scenario is unfamiliar.
pages.authorityGuidePages.encourageMobil: Encourage mobility, monitor for renal calculi, strain urine
pages.authorityGuidePages.equalStrengthB: Equal strength bilaterally, sensation intact, gait/balance
pages.authorityGuidePages.excessiveCoEli: Excessive CO₂ elimination due to hyperventilation. Loss of CO₂ (acid) raises pH.
pages.authorityGuidePages.facialSymmetry: Facial symmetry (CN VII), swallowing (CN IX/X), speech clarity
pages.authorityGuidePages.fastFaceDroopi: FAST (Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time) + NIH Stroke Scale, time of symptom onset
pages.authorityGuidePages.firstlineForDe: — first-line for dehydration, blood transfusions, and shock resuscitation. Does not shift fluid between compartments.
pages.authorityGuidePages.flattenedTWave: Flattened T waves, ST depression, prominent U waves, prolonged QT interval
pages.authorityGuidePages.fluidRestricti: Fluid restriction (dilutional hyponatremia / SIADH) — typically 800-1000 mL/day
pages.authorityGuidePages.focusedAssessm: Focused assessments are targeted evaluations performed when you know (or suspect) a specific condition. Exam questions often present a clinical scenario and ask "Which assessment is the priority?" Knowing the focused assessment for each condition is essential.
pages.authorityGuidePages.forDiabetesIns: For diabetes insipidus: administer desmopressin (DDAVP) as ordered
pages.authorityGuidePages.forEachElectro: For each electrolyte, know the single most important nursing action:
pages.authorityGuidePages.gcsTrendingPup: GCS trending, pupil reactivity and equality, Cushing
pages.authorityGuidePages.hcoDecreases2M: HCO₃⁻ decreases 2 mEq/L for every 10 mmHg fall in CO₂
pages.authorityGuidePages.hcoIncreases1M: HCO₃⁻ increases 1 mEq/L for every 10 mmHg rise in CO₂
pages.authorityGuidePages.hcoIncreases35: HCO₃⁻ increases 3.5 mEq/L for every 10 mmHg rise in CO₂
pages.authorityGuidePages.headacheConfus: Headache, confusion, drowsiness progressing to coma, warm flushed skin (CO₂ vasodilation), tachycardia, dysrhythmias. In chronic COPD: elevated HCO₃⁻ indicates renal compensation.
pages.authorityGuidePages.hyperaldostero: Hyperaldosteronism (sodium retention, H⁺ and K⁺ excretion)
pages.authorityGuidePages.hyperparathyro: Hyperparathyroidism, malignancy (bone metastases), prolonged immobilization, excessive vitamin D, thiazide diuretics
pages.authorityGuidePages.hypertonicSali: Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl) for severe symptomatic hyponatremia with seizures — administer via infusion pump with frequent Na⁺ monitoring
pages.authorityGuidePages.hypoparathyroi: Hypoparathyroidism, chronic kidney disease, pancreatitis, inadequate vitamin D, hypomagnesemia, massive blood transfusions (citrate binds calcium), thyroidectomy
pages.authorityGuidePages.hypotensionBra: Hypotension + Bradycardia (differs from other shock types which show tachycardia)
pages.authorityGuidePages.hypotensionTac: Hypotension + Tachycardia + Tachypnea + Altered mental status → immediate intervention
pages.authorityGuidePages.ifHypoxiadrive: If hypoxia-driven: treat the hypoxemia (oxygen therapy, PE management)
pages.authorityGuidePages.ifTheActualCom: If the actual compensation is more or less than expected, a mixed disorder is present. For example, a patient with DKA (metabolic acidosis) and pneumonia (respiratory acidosis) would have both low HCO₃⁻ AND high CO₂ — the pH would be very low because both systems are contributing to acidosis.
pages.authorityGuidePages.inClinicalPrac: In clinical practice and on advanced exam questions, patients may have two or more acid-base disorders simultaneously. Recognizing mixed disorders requires understanding expected compensation ranges.
pages.authorityGuidePages.inFullyCompens: In fully compensated ABGs, the pH is normal but both CO₂ and HCO₃⁻ are abnormal. To determine the primary disorder, look at which side of 7.40 the pH falls. If pH is 7.35-7.39, the primary disorder is acidosis. If pH is 7.41-7.45, the primary disorder is alkalosis.
pages.authorityGuidePages.includeRelevan: Include relevant negatives. "No JVD, no peripheral edema" is clinically important information
pages.authorityGuidePages.increasedIcpHy: (increased ICP): Hypertension (widening pulse pressure) + Bradycardia + Irregular respirations → neurosurgical emergency
pages.authorityGuidePages.increasedIntra: Increased intracranial pressure (worsens cerebral edema), burns (third-spacing), hypovolemia
pages.authorityGuidePages.inspectAuscult: Inspect, Auscultate, Percuss, Palpate (auscultate BEFORE palpation to avoid stimulating bowel sounds)
pages.authorityGuidePages.intenseThirstD: Intense thirst, dry mucous membranes, flushed skin, low-grade fever, restlessness, irritability, muscle twitching, seizures (severe)
pages.authorityGuidePages.isTheOtherValu: Is the other value also abnormal? If yes, the body is compensating. If the other value is normal, the disorder is uncompensated.
pages.authorityGuidePages.isotonicInTheB: — isotonic in the bag, becomes hypotonic once dextrose is metabolized. Used for medication dilution and maintenance hydration.
pages.authorityGuidePages.isotonicSoluti: Isotonic Solutions (Same osmolality as blood: ~275-295 mOsm/L)
pages.authorityGuidePages.ivCalciumGluco: IV calcium gluconate + airway equipment at bedside
pages.authorityGuidePages.ivFluidResusci: IV fluid resuscitation (isotonic crystalloid for DKA and sepsis)
pages.authorityGuidePages.ivMagnesiumSul: IV magnesium sulfate for severe depletion — administer slowly, monitor DTRs, respiratory rate, and urine output
pages.authorityGuidePages.ivNormalSaline: IV normal saline for chloride-responsive alkalosis (restores volume and chloride)
IV normal saline hydration (aggressive) — dilutes calcium and promotes renal excretion
pages.authorityGuidePages.ivPotassiumAlw: IV potassium: always diluted, always via pump, never by push
pages.authorityGuidePages.ivPotassiumNev: IV potassium: NEVER give by IV push — maximum rate 10-20 mEq/hour via infusion pump with cardiac monitoring
pages.authorityGuidePages.ivPotassiumPus: IV potassium push causes fatal cardiac arrest. This is one of the most commonly tested safety questions on the NCLEX. Always administer IV potassium diluted and infused slowly with cardiac monitoring. Never add KCl to a hanging IV bag — always use premixed solutions or infusion pump.
pages.authorityGuidePages.ivSodiumOrPota: IV sodium or potassium phosphate for severe deficiency — infuse slowly
pages.authorityGuidePages.kayexalateSodi: Kayexalate (sodium polystyrene sulfonate) — exchanges sodium for potassium in the gut (delayed onset)
pages.authorityGuidePages.keepEmergencyA: Keep emergency airway equipment at bedside (risk of laryngospasm)
pages.authorityGuidePages.kussmaulRespir: Kussmaul respirations (deep rapid breathing — respiratory compensation), fruity breath (DKA), confusion, lethargy, warm flushed skin, nausea/vomiting, hyperkalemia (H⁺/K⁺ exchange)
pages.authorityGuidePages.lightheadednes: Lightheadedness, dizziness, tingling/numbness (circumoral and extremities), muscle cramps, tetany (alkalosis decreases ionized calcium), palpitations, syncope.
pages.authorityGuidePages.loopAndThiazid: Loop and thiazide diuretics (most common), vomiting, NG suction, diarrhea, alkalosis, insulin administration, excessive laxative use
pages.authorityGuidePages.loopDiureticsF: Loop diuretics (furosemide) after adequate hydration — promotes calciuresis
pages.authorityGuidePages.lowphosphorusD: Low-phosphorus diet: limit dairy, cola, processed meats, nuts
pages.authorityGuidePages.magnesiumIsCri: Magnesium is critical for neuromuscular function and is closely linked to potassium and calcium balance. Hypomagnesemia must be corrected before hypokalemia will respond to treatment — this is a high-yield exam concept.
pages.authorityGuidePages.measureBpAndHr: Measure BP and HR supine, then standing (or sitting if unable to stand). Positive orthostatic hypotension: SBP drop ≥ 20 mmHg OR DBP drop ≥ 10 mmHg OR HR increase ≥ 20 bpm within 3 minutes. Indicates volume depletion or autonomic dysfunction. Nursing action: ensure safety (fall precautions), report to provider, hold antihypertensives as appropriate.
pages.authorityGuidePages.mechanicalVent: Mechanical ventilation (rate or tidal volume set too high)
Mechanical ventilation: reduce rate or tidal volume as ordered
pages.authorityGuidePages.monitorDtrsBef: Monitor DTRs before each magnesium sulfate dose (hold if absent)
pages.authorityGuidePages.monitorEcgCont: Monitor ECG continuously during IV replacement
pages.authorityGuidePages.monitorEcgForD: Monitor ECG for dysrhythmias related to hypokalemia
pages.authorityGuidePages.monitorForAsso: Monitor for associated hypocalcemia and hypomagnesemia
pages.authorityGuidePages.monitorForHypo: Monitor for hypoventilation and hypoxemia (respiratory compensation)
pages.authorityGuidePages.monitorForTeta: Monitor for tetany and cardiac dysrhythmias (alkalosis-related hypocalcemia)
pages.authorityGuidePages.monitorNeuroSt: Monitor neuro status every 1-2 hours — confusion, lethargy, and seizures indicate worsening
pages.authorityGuidePages.monitorPotassi: Monitor potassium closely — as acidosis corrects, K⁺ shifts back into cells causing hypokalemia
pages.authorityGuidePages.monitorRespira: Monitor respiratory rate, depth, and pattern every 1-2 hours
pages.authorityGuidePages.monitorUrineSp: Monitor urine specific gravity (normal: 1.005-1.030) — low in DI, high in dehydration
pages.authorityGuidePages.muscleWeakness: Muscle weakness, leg cramps, decreased DTRs, constipation/ileus, fatigue, cardiac dysrhythmias
Muscle weakness progressing to flaccid paralysis, paresthesias, abdominal cramping, diarrhea, cardiac arrest
Muscle weakness, respiratory failure (diaphragm weakness), confusion, seizures, rhabdomyolysis, impaired WBC function
pages.authorityGuidePages.neuroChecksEve: Neuro checks every 1-2 hours, drainage (CSF vs blood), wound site, seizure precautions
pages.authorityGuidePages.neurovascularC: Neurovascular checks, abduction pillow/positioning, DVT prevention, pain management, mobility progression
pages.authorityGuidePages.newPressureInj: New pressure injury, signs of wound infection, crepitus (subcutaneous emphysema), cyanosis
pages.authorityGuidePages.newonsetAcuteF: New-onset (acute) findings generally take priority over chronic/stable findings. A patient with new-onset confusion is a higher priority than a patient with known chronic pain, even if the chronic pain patient is uncomfortable.
pages.authorityGuidePages.normal530minIn: Normal: 5-30/min in all four quadrants. Hyperactive: diarrhea, early obstruction. Hypoactive/absent: ileus, post-operative
pages.authorityGuidePages.oftenConsidere: Often considered the "5th vital sign." Use appropriate scale for patient population.
pages.authorityGuidePages.onDocumentatio: On documentation questions, choose the most objective, specific, and measurable option. Avoid answers that include nursing opinions, subjective interpretations, or vague terms like "good," "normal," or "improved" without measurable criteria.
pages.authorityGuidePages.opioidOverdose: Opioid overdose: administer naloxone (Narcan) — monitor for re-sedation
pages.authorityGuidePages.opioidsedative: Opioid/sedative overdose, pneumothorax, airway obstruction, neuromuscular disease (Guillain-Barré, myasthenia gravis), severe asthma, pneumonia, ARDS, anesthesia
pages.authorityGuidePages.oralCalciumSup: Oral calcium supplements with vitamin D for chronic management
pages.authorityGuidePages.oralPotassiumR: Oral potassium replacement preferred (KCl tablets with full glass of water and food to reduce GI irritation)
pages.authorityGuidePages.painAssessment: Pain assessment (PQRST), vital signs, ECG monitoring, troponin levels, O₂ saturation
pages.authorityGuidePages.peakFlowMeasur: Peak flow measurement, work of breathing, breath sounds (wheezing, silent chest = severe), SpO₂, ability to speak in full sentences
pages.authorityGuidePages.perrlaPupilsEq: PERRLA (Pupils Equal, Round, Reactive to Light and Accommodation) — unequal or fixed/dilated = emergency
pages.authorityGuidePages.personPlaceTim: Person, place, time, situation (documented as "A&Ox4" or partial)
pages.authorityGuidePages.ph728Paco55Hco: pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 55, HCO₃⁻ 24 → Uncompensated respiratory acidosis (pH low, CO₂ high, HCO₃⁻ normal). Think: COPD exacerbation, opioid overdose, or pneumonia.
pages.authorityGuidePages.ph732Paco30Hco: pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 30, HCO₃⁻ 15 → Partially compensated metabolic acidosis (pH low, HCO₃⁻ low, CO₂ low from respiratory compensation). Think: DKA, lactic acidosis, or renal failure.
pages.authorityGuidePages.ph748Paco28Hco: pH 7.48, PaCO₂ 28, HCO₃⁻ 24 → Uncompensated respiratory alkalosis (pH high, CO₂ low, HCO₃⁻ normal). Think: anxiety, pain, PE, or early sepsis.
pages.authorityGuidePages.ph750Paco46Hco: pH 7.50, PaCO₂ 46, HCO₃⁻ 34 → Partially compensated metabolic alkalosis (pH high, HCO₃⁻ high, CO₂ slightly elevated from respiratory compensation). Think: vomiting, NG suctioning, or diuretic use.
pages.authorityGuidePages.phIsAbnormalOn: pH is abnormal, only one value (CO₂ or HCO₃⁻) is abnormal. The body has not yet begun to compensate.
pages.authorityGuidePages.phIsDetermined: pH is determined by the ratio of HCO₃⁻ to CO₂. Normal ratio is 20:1 (bicarbonate to carbonic acid). Any process that changes this ratio will alter the pH.
pages.authorityGuidePages.phIsStillAbnor: pH is still abnormal, but both CO₂ and HCO₃⁻ are abnormal. The body is trying to compensate but has not fully corrected the pH.
pages.authorityGuidePages.phIsWithinNorm: pH is within normal range (7.35-7.45), but both CO₂ and HCO₃⁻ are abnormal. Look at which end of normal the pH falls to determine the primary disorder.
pages.authorityGuidePages.phosphatebindi: Phosphate-binding medications (calcium carbonate, sevelamer) — administer WITH meals to bind dietary phosphorus
pages.authorityGuidePages.phosphorusHasA: Phosphorus has an inverse relationship with calcium — when one goes up, the other goes down. This reciprocal relationship is a frequently tested concept. Phosphorus is essential for ATP production, bone mineralization, and acid-base buffering.
pages.authorityGuidePages.potassiumIsThe: Potassium is the most abundant intracellular cation and is critical for cardiac conduction, neuromuscular function, and acid-base balance. Both hypokalemia and hyperkalemia can cause life-threatening cardiac dysrhythmias, making potassium the most frequently tested electrolyte on nursing exams. ECG changes are a priority assessment finding.
pages.authorityGuidePages.practiceWithEx: Practice with exam-style questions and detailed clinical rationales.
pages.authorityGuidePages.preferredForSu: — preferred for surgical patients and burns (Parkland formula). Contains electrolytes (Na, K, Ca, lactate). Contraindicated in liver failure (cannot metabolize lactate) and hyperkalemia.
pages.authorityGuidePages.prepareForPoss: Prepare for possible intubation/mechanical ventilation if PaCO₂ continues to rise
pages.authorityGuidePages.rate1220minDep: Rate (12-20/min), depth, pattern (regular, Cheyne-Stokes, Kussmaul, Biot
pages.authorityGuidePages.readTheLabValu: Read the lab values carefully. Identify which electrolyte is abnormal and whether it is high or low. Many students make errors because they focus on the clinical scenario before checking the labs.
pages.authorityGuidePages.refeedingSyndr: Refeeding syndrome is a life-threatening complication of rapidly restarting nutrition in malnourished patients. As cells take up glucose, phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium shift intracellularly causing dangerous drops. This is heavily tested in context of eating disorder patients and prolonged NPO status.
Refeeding syndrome (most commonly tested), chronic alcoholism, diabetic ketoacidosis recovery (insulin drives PO₄ into cells), phosphate-binding antacids, respiratory alkalosis
Refeeding syndrome prevention: advance nutrition gradually in malnourished patients
pages.authorityGuidePages.regularInsulin: Regular insulin + D50 IV — drives K⁺ into cells (temporary, monitor glucose closely)
pages.authorityGuidePages.regulateCoLeve: Regulate CO₂ levels within minutes. Hyperventilation blows off CO₂ (raises pH). Hypoventilation retains CO₂ (lowers pH). CO₂ is an acid when dissolved in blood (forms carbonic acid, H₂CO₃).
pages.authorityGuidePages.regulateHcoBic: Regulate HCO₃⁻ (bicarbonate) levels over hours to days. The kidneys can either retain or excrete bicarbonate and hydrogen ions. This is a slower but more sustained compensatory mechanism.
pages.authorityGuidePages.reinforceKeyCo: Reinforce key concepts with spaced-repetition flashcards.
pages.authorityGuidePages.rememberStones: Remember "stones, bones, groans, moans" — kidney stones, bone pain, abdominal pain/constipation, confusion/lethargy. Shortened QT, cardiac arrest at severe levels.
pages.authorityGuidePages.renalFailureCa: Renal failure (cannot excrete), excessive magnesium-containing antacids/laxatives, preeclampsia treatment (magnesium sulfate toxicity)
Renal failure (cannot excrete), excessive phosphorus intake, hypoparathyroidism, tumor lysis syndrome, rhabdomyolysis
pages.authorityGuidePages.renalFailureMo: Renal failure (most common), potassium-sparing diuretics, ACE inhibitors, tissue trauma/burns, acidosis, hemolyzed blood sample (false elevation)
pages.authorityGuidePages.replacePotassi: Replace potassium (hypokalemia is both a cause and consequence of metabolic alkalosis)
Replace potassium (NEVER IV push) + monitor ECG
pages.authorityGuidePages.respiratorySta: Respiratory status (airway swelling), voice quality, Chvostek
pages.authorityGuidePages.rigidboardlike: Rigid/board-like abdomen, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds, hematemesis, melena
pages.authorityGuidePages.s1Mitraltricus: S1 (mitral/tricuspid closure), S2 (aortic/pulmonic closure), S3 (HF indicator in adults), S4 (decreased compliance), murmurs
pages.authorityGuidePages.scale1To4Pitti: Scale 1+ to 4+ pitting, location, onset pattern (bilateral vs unilateral)
pages.authorityGuidePages.shallowSlowRes: Shallow slow respirations (compensatory hypoventilation), confusion, irritability, muscle cramps, tetany (alkalosis decreases ionized calcium), hypokalemia (H⁺/K⁺ exchange), cardiac dysrhythmias
pages.authorityGuidePages.siadhMostCommo: SIADH (most common exam cause), excessive hypotonic IV fluids, heart failure, cirrhosis, psychogenic polydipsia, thiazide diuretics, vomiting/diarrhea with water replacement
pages.authorityGuidePages.signsOfBleedin: Signs of bleeding (bruising, gum bleeding, dark stools, hematuria), PTT/INR levels
pages.authorityGuidePages.similarToHypoc: Similar to hypocalcemia — tremors, hyperactive DTRs, positive Trousseau
pages.authorityGuidePages.situationBackg: Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation — used for provider communication
pages.authorityGuidePages.sizeDepthDrain: Size, depth, drainage (serous, sanguineous, purulent), wound bed color, periwound skin
pages.authorityGuidePages.sodiumBicarbon: Sodium bicarbonate IV — shifts K⁺ intracellularly in metabolic acidosis
pages.authorityGuidePages.sodiumIsTheMos: Sodium is the most abundant extracellular cation and the primary determinant of serum osmolality. Sodium imbalances are fundamentally water balance disorders — hyponatremia usually reflects water excess, while hypernatremia reflects water deficit.
pages.authorityGuidePages.softNontenderN: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Rebound tenderness = peritoneal irritation (emergency)
pages.authorityGuidePages.somethingIsAdd: Something is adding acid to the body:
pages.authorityGuidePages.stage1Nonblanc: Stage 1 (non-blanchable erythema) through Stage 4 (full-thickness with exposed bone/tendon), Unstageable, DTPI
pages.authorityGuidePages.strategy2Match: Strategy 2: Match Signs to the Imbalance
pages.authorityGuidePages.strictIoDailyW: Strict I&O, daily weights, safety measures for confusion
Strict I&O, daily weights, seizure precautions
pages.authorityGuidePages.subjectiveObje: Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan — problem-oriented charting
pages.authorityGuidePages.suddenUnilater: Sudden unilateral weakness, slurred speech, severe headache, pupil changes, decreasing GCS
pages.authorityGuidePages.tallPeakedTWav: Tall peaked T waves → widened QRS → absent P waves → sine wave pattern → ventricular fibrillation/asystole
pages.authorityGuidePages.temperatureHyp: Temperature (hyperthermia), HR (severe tachycardia), agitation, delirium, diaphoresis
pages.authorityGuidePages.theMostFundame: The most fundamental prioritization framework. Always address airway problems first, then breathing, then circulation. On the NCLEX, if one patient has an airway issue and another has a circulation issue, the airway patient is always the priority.
pages.authorityGuidePages.theNclexLovesP: The NCLEX loves patient safety questions involving electrolytes:
pages.authorityGuidePages.theRomeMethodI: The ROME method is the most reliable systematic approach to ABG interpretation on nursing exams. It eliminates guesswork and works for every ABG scenario.
pages.authorityGuidePages.theStudyGuideY: The study guide you are looking for does not exist.
pages.authorityGuidePages.toSucceedWithE: To succeed with electrolyte questions, you need to understand three things for each electrolyte: (1) the normal range and what drives it up or down, (2) the clinical signs and symptoms of both excess and deficit, and (3) the priority nursing interventions including monitoring, medication administration, and patient safety measures.
pages.authorityGuidePages.treatTheUnderl: Treat the underlying cause: antiemetics for vomiting, replace NG losses with appropriate fluids
Treat the underlying cause (insulin for DKA, antibiotics for sepsis, dialysis for renal failure)
pages.authorityGuidePages.treatUnderlyin: Treat underlying cause: analgesics for pain, antipyretics for fever, anxiolytics for anxiety
pages.authorityGuidePages.understandingI: Understanding IV fluid tonicity is essential for managing electrolyte imbalances safely. Selecting the wrong IV fluid can worsen the imbalance and cause harm — this is why IV fluid questions are common on nursing exams.
pages.authorityGuidePages.unexpectedFind: Unexpected findings require action; expected findings require monitoring. If a post-surgical patient reports mild incisional pain (expected), that is lower priority than a patient reporting sudden chest tightness (unexpected). Know what is expected for each condition so you can recognize the unexpected.
pages.authorityGuidePages.unstablePatien: Unstable patients always take priority. Stable patients can wait, can often be delegated, and are appropriate for nursing assistants to monitor. Unstable patients require RN assessment and intervention.
pages.authorityGuidePages.useApprovedAbb: Use approved abbreviations only. If you make an error, draw a single line through it, write "error," initial, and date — never use white-out or scribble over
pages.authorityGuidePages.useMeasurableT: Use measurable terms. "Wound 3 cm x 2 cm with serous drainage" is better than "wound looks good"
pages.authorityGuidePages.usedForHyperna: — used for hypernatremia, cellular dehydration. Fluid shifts FROM vascular space INTO cells.
pages.authorityGuidePages.usedForSevereS: — used for severe symptomatic hyponatremia with seizures. Fluid shifts FROM cells INTO vascular space.
pages.authorityGuidePages.viaInfusionPum: Via infusion pump only, central line preferred, frequent sodium monitoring (every 2-4 hours)
pages.authorityGuidePages.vitalSignsAreT: Vital signs are the most basic and most critical assessment data. Knowing normal ranges, understanding what deviations mean, and recognizing life-threatening vital sign patterns is essential for both exam success and clinical practice.
pages.authorityGuidePages.vomitingngSuct: Vomiting/NG suctioning (loss of HCl from stomach — most commonly tested)
pages.authorityGuidePages.whenAnExamQues: When an exam question presents multiple patients with abnormal vital signs, use the ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) to prioritize. A patient with respiratory distress (SpO₂ 82%, RR 32) takes priority over a patient with fever (38.8°C) or mild tachycardia (HR 105).
pages.authorityGuidePages.whichAssessmen: — "Which assessment finding indicates..." or "The nurse should assess for which complication?"
— "Which assessment finding requires immediate intervention?" or "Which patient should the nurse see first?"
pages.authorityGuidePages.whichValueCoOr: Which value (CO₂ or HCO₃⁻) explains the pH direction? That is the primary disorder.

pages.healthcarePolicyPages.canadianNurs: Canadian nursing exams, including the REX-PN and NCLEX-RN (used by most Canadian provinces for RN licensure), follow similar CAT methodologies but may have jurisdiction-specific content emphases. The transition from the CPNRE to the REX-PN for practical nursing marked a significant format shift toward the CAT model.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.catTechnolog: CAT technology ensures each candidate receives a uniquely tailored exam. Understanding how CAT works helps reduce test anxiety and inform your test-taking strategy.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.commonPathwa: Common pathways include employer-sponsored work visas, skilled worker programs (such as Canada
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.comprehensiv: Comprehensive guide to healthcare regulatory changes affecting nursing practice. Covers staffing ratio laws, patient safety regulations, telehealth standards, continuing education mandates, and workplace safety requirements.

Comprehensive guide to nursing licensure policy changes including Nurse Licensure Compact updates, scope of practice changes, endorsement requirements, and license renewal policies across jurisdictions.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.continuingEd: Continuing education requirements are integral to license renewal. Regulatory bodies periodically update these requirements to address emerging healthcare challenges, new evidence-based practices, and evolving patient safety standards.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.eachDestinat: Each destination country has established organizations responsible for evaluating the credentials of internationally educated nurses. These evaluations assess whether your nursing education and clinical experience meet local standards.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.guideToInter: Guide to international nursing recruitment policies including credential evaluation processes, visa and immigration pathways, bridging program requirements, and recruitment agency regulations for internationally educated nurses.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.healthcareRe: Healthcare regulations shape every aspect of nursing practice. Understanding regulatory developments helps you maintain compliance, advocate for your profession, and deliver safe patient care.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.healthcareWo: Healthcare workplace safety regulations address unique risks faced by nurses including exposure to infectious diseases, workplace violence, musculoskeletal injuries from patient handling, and psychological stressors. Recent legislative trends include stronger protections against workplace violence and mandatory break periods.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.internationa: International nursing recruitment involves navigating immigration systems that are designed to address healthcare workforce shortages. Many countries have created specialized visa categories for healthcare professionals, recognizing the critical role nurses play in their healthcare systems.
International Nursing Recruitment Policies: Credential Evaluation, Visa Pathways & Bridging Programs | NurseNest
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.manyJurisdic: Many jurisdictions require internationally educated nurses to complete bridging programs that address gaps between their training and local practice standards. These programs typically include supervised clinical placements, classroom instruction on local healthcare systems, and competency assessments.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.navigateTheP: Navigate the policies and pathways that govern international nursing recruitment. From credential evaluation to visa processing, understand the framework for cross-border nursing careers.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.nurseStaffin: Nurse staffing regulations are among the most impactful policy developments in healthcare. Research consistently demonstrates the relationship between nurse staffing levels and patient outcomes, including mortality rates, hospital-acquired infections, patient falls, and medication errors.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.nursingExamF: Nursing Exam Format Updates: NGN, CAT Changes, Score Reporting & Testing Policies | NurseNest
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.nursingExami: Nursing examinations evolve to better measure clinical competency. Understanding format changes, new item types, and scoring methodologies helps you prepare effectively.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.nursingLicen: Nursing Licensing Policy Changes: Compact Licenses, Scope of Practice & Licensure Updates | NurseNest
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.recentTrends: Recent trends include mandatory education on opioid prescribing and pain management, implicit bias and health equity training, infection prevention in the context of emerging diseases, and suicide prevention assessment competencies.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.regulatoryBo: Regulatory bodies continuously update safety standards based on evidence-based practice. Key areas include medication safety (barcode scanning, smart pump technology), infection prevention protocols, fall prevention programs, pressure injury prevention, and safe patient handling requirements.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.regulatoryCh: Regulatory Changes Affecting Nurses: Staffing Laws, Safety Standards & Practice Requirements | NurseNest
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.scopeOfPract: Scope of practice laws determine what nursing professionals can legally do in their practice. These laws are evolving across jurisdictions, particularly for nurse practitioners and advanced practice nurses.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.scoreReporti: Score reporting processes have been streamlined in recent years. NCLEX results are typically available within 48 hours through Quick Results, with official results sent to the candidate
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.stayCurrentW: Stay current with nursing exam format changes. Comprehensive guide covering Next Generation NCLEX updates, computerized adaptive testing changes, score reporting modifications, and testing center policies.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.telehealthRe: Telehealth regulations have evolved rapidly, creating new opportunities and challenges for nursing practice. Key regulatory considerations include cross-state practice authority, informed consent for virtual care, documentation requirements, and scope of practice in telehealth settings.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.theMinimumNu: The minimum number of questions varies by exam: NCLEX-RN requires a minimum of 85 items (maximum 150), while the NCLEX-PN requires a minimum of 85 items (maximum 150). The exam ends when the algorithm reaches sufficient confidence in the pass/fail decision or when the maximum number of items is reached.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.theNextGener: The Next Generation NCLEX represents the most significant change to nursing licensure examinations in decades. NGN introduces clinical judgment-focused item types that go beyond traditional multiple-choice questions to assess how nurses think through complex patient scenarios.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.theNurseLice: The Nurse Licensure Compact is one of the most significant policy developments in US nursing. It allows nurses holding a multistate license to practice in any compact member state without obtaining additional licenses. This has dramatically improved workforce mobility and disaster response capabilities.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.theWhoGlobal: The WHO Global Code of Practice on the International Recruitment of Health Personnel provides a framework for ethical recruitment. Key principles include transparency in recruitment practices, fair treatment of internationally recruited staff, sustainability of health systems in source countries, and prohibition of charging recruitment fees to candidates.
pages.healthcarePolicyPages.understandin: Understanding how licensure policies evolve is essential for maintaining your credentials and planning career moves across jurisdictions. This guide covers the major policy frameworks affecting nursing licenses.

pages.herbalSupplementsHub.asaRecommends: ASA recommends stopping all herbals 2-3 weeks before surgery. Valerian must be tapered (not stopped abruptly — withdrawal risk). If the question says surgery is tomorrow and the patient just disclosed valerian use, correct answer: notify the surgeon.
pages.herbalSupplementsHub.askOpenendedW: Ask open-ended: "What supplements, vitamins, or herbal products do you take?"
pages.herbalSupplementsHub.comprehensive: Comprehensive nursing guide to herbal supplements and medication interactions. Learn about the 4 Gs, CYP450 interactions, perioperative safety, and patient teaching for NCLEX success.
pages.herbalSupplementsHub.flagIdentifie: Flag identified interactions for prescriber review
pages.herbalSupplementsHub.ginkgoGarlicG: Ginkgo, Garlic, Ginger, Ginseng — all increase bleeding risk. Exam writers love to test a patient on warfarin who is also taking one of these. Correct answer: notify the prescriber about additive bleeding risk.
pages.herbalSupplementsHub.includeTeasTo: Include teas, topical products, and homeopathic remedies
pages.herbalSupplementsHub.over50OfAdult: Over 50% of adults use supplements but fewer than 40% tell their providers. When asked the most effective assessment question, choose the open-ended, non-judgmental one: "What vitamins, supplements, or herbal products do you take?"

pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.05Mlkg: 0.5 mL/kg/hr over last 4 hours (borderline — normal minimum for children: 1 mL/kg/hr).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.20Mlkg: 20 mL/kg NS bolus (14 kg × 20 = 280 mL) over 5–10 minutes. Reassess after each bolus — may repeat up to 60 mL/kg in the first hour. Assess for fluid responsiveness: improved heart rate, perfusion, mental status, urine output.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.30Mlkg: 30 mL/kg crystalloid (typically NS or LR) within 3 hours. For this 65 kg patient: ~2000 mL bolus. Reassess fluid responsiveness after each bolus.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.5IfMap: 5. If MAP remains < 65 mmHg after fluids:
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.activa: Activate rapid response or pediatric emergency team. This child shows signs of compensated septic shock — do not wait for further deterioration.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.acuteE: Acute exacerbation of COPD (AECOPD) with acute-on-chronic type II respiratory failure. Yellow-green sputum and mild leukocytosis suggest an infectious trigger (likely bacterial bronchitis or pneumonia).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.acuteI: Acute ischemic stroke — likely cardioembolic origin (atrial fibrillation with subtherapeutic anticoagulation). Left middle cerebral artery (MCA) territory based on right hemiparesis, facial droop, and aphasia pattern.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.acuteK: Acute kidney injury (creatinine 1.6 from baseline 0.9), altered mental status, hypotension, elevated lactate — multiple organ systems are showing dysfunction consistent with sepsis.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.acuteP: Acute pericarditis (diffuse ST elevation, no reciprocal changes), aortic dissection (tearing pain, unequal pulses), pulmonary embolism (pleuritic pain, tachycardia, hypoxia).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.admitt: Admitted 18 hours ago with community-acquired pneumonia. Night nurse reports the child
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.alertB: Alert but confused, follows simple commands inconsistently. Right hemiparesis (arm greater than leg). Right-sided facial droop (lower face). Receptive language partially intact but expressive aphasia present. Right visual field neglect suspected.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.alread: (already obtained: 3.8 mmol/L). Repeat lactate in 2–4 hours. Target: lactate clearance or decrease by ≥ 10%.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.anxiou: Anxious, diaphoretic, clutching chest. Skin pale and clammy.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.atrial: Atrial fibrillation (on warfarin, inconsistent compliance), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bilate: Bilateral breath sounds clear, no crackles. Mild tachypnea.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bloodC: Blood cultures × 2, CBC with differential, CRP, lactate, BMP (electrolytes, glucose, renal function), blood gas. Obtain before changing antibiotics if possible.
Blood cultures × 2 (from different sites), urine culture, wound culture from surgical site. Do not delay antibiotics more than 45 minutes for cultures.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bornAt: Born at 35 weeks gestation, no significant neonatal complications. Up to date on immunizations. No chronic conditions. No allergies.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bp1488: BP 148/88 mmHg, HR 110 bpm, RR 32/min, SpO₂ 86% on room air, Temp 37.8°C
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bp1589: BP 158/94 mmHg, HR 102 bpm (regular), RR 22/min, SpO₂ 96% on room air, Temp 37.1°C
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bp1861: BP 186/102 mmHg, HR 88 bpm (irregularly irregular), RR 18/min, SpO₂ 97%, Temp 36.8°C, Blood glucose 142 mg/dL
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.bp8852: BP 88/52 mmHg (baseline 130/78), HR 118 bpm, RR 24/min, SpO₂ 93% on room air, Temp 39.2°C (102.6°F)
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.cardia: Cardiac monitoring, continuous pulse oximetry, strict I&O (weigh diapers), frequent vital signs q15min during acute resuscitation, blood glucose monitoring (children are prone to hypoglycemia during sepsis).
Cardiac telemetry, serial ECGs, repeat troponin at 3 and 6 hours, strict I&O.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.cefazo: Cefazolin 2g IV q8h (surgical prophylaxis), enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, oxycodone 5 mg PO q4h PRN, metformin (held post-op), lisinopril 10 mg daily.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.childr: Children compensate for shock much longer than adults, maintaining blood pressure until very late. By the time hypotension occurs in a child, they are in decompensated shock — a pre-arrest state. The key is recognizing early signs: tachycardia, altered mental status, poor perfusion, and decreased urine output.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.confus: Confused, restless, appears ill. Skin warm and flushed with poor turgor.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.contin: Continuous nebulized albuterol 2.5 mg + ipratropium 0.5 mg q20min × 3, then q4h. Consider adding IV magnesium sulfate if severe bronchospasm.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.copdDi: COPD (diagnosed 8 years ago), 40 pack-year smoking history (current smoker), hypertension, anxiety. Two prior COPD exacerbations requiring hospitalization in the past year.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.ctComp: CT completed within 10 minutes of arrival (achieved). Rule out hemorrhagic stroke before considering thrombolytics.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.decrea: Decreased cardiac output related to myocardial ischemia/infarction as evidenced by ST elevation, elevated troponin, chest pain, tachycardia, and diaphoresis.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.dimini: Diminished breath sounds bilaterally with scattered expiratory wheezes and rhonchi. Prolonged expiratory phase. Barrel chest configuration. No stridor.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.doorto: — door-to-balloon time goal < 90 minutes for primary PCI.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.dose09: Dose: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg). Give 10% as IV bolus over 1 minute, then infuse remaining 90% over 60 minutes. Start within 60 minutes of arrival.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.explai: Explain procedures, provide emotional support, keep family informed.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.faceLe: Face — left-sided facial droop when smiling. Arms — right arm drifts downward and cannot maintain against gravity. Speech — dysarthric, word-finding difficulty, unable to repeat sentences accurately. Time — symptom onset 30 minutes ago.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.foleyC: Foley catheter in place — urine cloudy and concentrated, output 15 mL in last 2 hours.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.forTpa: For tPA candidates, BP must be < 185/110 before and < 180/105 during/after tPA. Current BP 186/102 — administer IV labetalol 10–20 mg to lower BP below threshold.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.hepari: Heparin bolus + drip per protocol, clopidogrel/ticagrelor loading dose, beta-blocker (metoprolol) if HR > 100 and no contraindications.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.hr168B: HR 168 bpm (normal for age: 80–120), RR 42/min (normal: 20–30), SpO₂ 90% on 2L nasal cannula (was 96% on admission), Temp 39.8°C (103.6°F), BP 78/45 mmHg (normal minimum SBP: 70 + [2 × age] = 76 mmHg — borderline low)
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.hypert: Hypertension (10 years), hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, former smoker (quit 5 years ago), family history of MI (father at age 58).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.iHaveA: "I have a crushing pain in my chest that won
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.increa: Increase to high-flow nasal cannula or non-rebreather mask to target SpO₂ > 94%. Position upright. Be prepared for respiratory support (CPAP, intubation) if grunting worsens or mental status declines further.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.initia: Initiate empiric antibiotics (e.g., azithromycin or levofloxacin) given purulent sputum and suspected bacterial trigger.
Initiate vasopressor therapy (norepinephrine first-line). May require ICU transfer.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.inr16S: INR 1.6 (subtherapeutic — target 2.0–3.0 on warfarin), glucose 142 mg/dL, CBC within normal limits.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.keepNp: Keep NPO until bedside swallow evaluation completed. Dysphagia screening is mandatory before any oral intake (aspiration risk).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.keepPa: Keep parents informed and at bedside when possible. Explain interventions and what to expect. Parents know their child best — their concern that the child "doesn
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.lastKn: Last known well time 30 minutes ago — well within the 4.5-hour window for IV alteplase. CT shows no hemorrhage. INR 1.6 (alteplase can be given if INR < 1.7). No recent surgery, bleeding, or other absolute contraindications identified.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.lisino: Lisinopril 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg BID.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.listle: Listless, not making eye contact, not interested in toys. Responds to voice but irritable when touched. Not crying with tears — mucous membranes tacky. Skin mottled on extremities, capillary refill 4 seconds.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.methyl: Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV or prednisone 40 mg PO. Reduces airway inflammation and shortens recovery time.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.miaWas: Mia was admitted with 3-day history of cough, fever, and decreased oral intake. Initial chest X-ray showed right lower lobe consolidation. Started on IV ampicillin. Was playful and interactive on admission but has become progressively less active over the past 6 hours. Parents report she has not eaten and has had minimal fluid intake.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.morphi: Morphine 2–4 mg IV PRN (if pain not relieved by NTG), Oxygen only if SpO₂ < 94%, Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL q5min × 3 (hold if SBP < 90), Aspirin 325 mg chewed (already taken by patient).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.neuroC: Neuro checks q15min × 2h, then q30min × 6h, then q1h × 16h. Strict BP monitoring — keep < 180/105. Monitor for signs of hemorrhagic conversion (new headache, vomiting, acute neuro decline).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.noHemo: No hemorrhage identified. No early ischemic changes.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.normal: Normal BNP rules out heart failure as a contributing factor. The clinical picture is consistent with infectious COPD exacerbation.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.notify: Notify neurologist/stroke team immediately. Prepare for IV alteplase (tPA) administration.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.nursin: Nursing Clinical Scenarios — Interactive Case Studies
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.obtain: Obtain before starting antibiotics if possible.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.osteoa: Osteoarthritis, hypertension, type 2 diabetes (A1C 7.8%), urinary tract infections (recurrent).
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.painSt: Pain started 45 minutes ago while watching television. Substernal, radiating to the left arm and jaw. Rates pain 8/10. Describes it as "heavy" and "squeezing." Associated with diaphoresis and nausea. Took one aspirin at home before calling 911.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.patien: Patient was at baseline 30 minutes ago. Wife reports he suddenly dropped his fork, his right arm went limp, and he began speaking incoherently. He is able to follow simple commands inconsistently. Last known well time: 12:15 PM. Current time: 12:45 PM.

Patient was progressing normally on POD 1 and 2. On POD 3 morning, she became increasingly confused and oriented only to person. Temperature spiked to 39.2°C. She reports feeling "cold" despite the fever. Surgical wound has increased redness and warmth around incision edges.

Patient with known COPD (GOLD Stage III) presents to the ED with worsening dyspnea over the past 3 days. Reports increased sputum production (yellow-green) and a productive cough. Used his rescue inhaler 8 times today with minimal relief. Cannot speak in full sentences. Was sitting in tripod position upon arrival.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.ph731A: pH 7.31 = acidosis. PaCO₂ 58 = elevated (respiratory acidosis). HCO₃ 32 = elevated (chronic compensation). PaO₂ 54 = severe hypoxemia. This is a partially compensated respiratory acidosis with acute-on-chronic respiratory failure. The elevated HCO₃ indicates pre-existing chronic CO₂ retention (baseline COPD) with acute worsening.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.ph731P: pH 7.31, PaCO₂ 58 mmHg, HCO₃ 32 mEq/L, PaO₂ 54 mmHg
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.postop: Post-operative day 3 following right total hip arthroplasty. Nurse noted increasing confusion and elevated temperature during routine assessment.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.practi: Practice clinical reasoning with interactive nursing scenarios. Chest pain, sepsis, respiratory distress, stroke, and pediatric deterioration cases with assessment findings, nursing priorities, and quiz questions.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.repeat: Repeat ABG in 1–2 hours. If PaCO₂ continues to rise, pH drops below 7.25, or mental status worsens → prepare for non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) or intubation. BiPAP is first-line for COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.rightH: Right hip incision with erythema extending 3 cm beyond staple line, warmth, and small amount of purulent drainage.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.s1s2He: S1/S2 heard, no murmurs. Regular tachycardia. No JVD.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.sittin: Sitting upright, tripod position, using accessory muscles (sternocleidomastoid, intercostals), pursed-lip breathing, speaks 2–3 word sentences only. Appears fatigued.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.stElev: ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF (2–3 mm). Reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.startO: Start O₂ via Venturi mask at 28% — titrate to target SpO₂ 88–92%. COPD patients rely on hypoxic drive; excessive O₂ can suppress respiratory drive and worsen CO₂ retention.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.strict: Strict I&O (urine output goal ≥ 0.5 mL/kg/hr), continuous cardiac monitoring, frequent vital signs (q15 min during resuscitation), neuro checks q1h.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.subcos: Subcostal and intercostal retractions, nasal flaring, grunting on expiration. Diminished breath sounds right lower lobe with crackles. Increased work of breathing compared to admission.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.surgic: Surgical site infection (SSI) with purulent drainage and expanding erythema. Concurrent UTI possible given cloudy urine, indwelling catheter, and history of recurrent UTIs. Both sources require cultures before broadening antibiotic coverage.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.tachyc: Tachycardic, weak peripheral pulses. Cool extremities despite fever.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.theCli: The clinical picture suggests progression from localized pneumonia to possible sepsis with impending septic shock. The hemodynamic changes (tachycardia, borderline BP, poor perfusion) suggest compensated shock — the body is maintaining blood pressure through tachycardia, but decompensation is imminent if not treated aggressively.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.theCur: The current regimen (ampicillin alone) may be insufficient. Notify the provider to consider broadening to ceftriaxone or adding vancomycin if MRSA is suspected.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.theStE: The ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) with reciprocal changes in lateral leads indicates an inferior STEMI. The elevated troponin confirms myocardial injury. Risk factors (age, male, HTN, DM, hyperlipidemia, smoking history, family history) create a high pretest probability for ACS. The 45-minute symptom duration places this patient within the PCI window.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.thisCh: This child is showing multiple red flags for clinical deterioration: rising heart rate, worsening respiratory distress (retractions, grunting, nasal flaring), declining oxygen saturation despite supplemental O₂, altered behavior (listless, not interactive), poor perfusion (mottled skin, prolonged cap refill, cool extremities, weak pulses), decreased urine output, and persistent high fever.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.thisPa: This patient meets SIRS criteria (temp > 38.3°C, HR > 90, RR > 20, WBC > 12,000) with a suspected infection source (surgical site and/or UTI). The qSOFA score of 2 (altered mentation + hypotension) indicates high risk for poor outcome. Elevated lactate (3.8 mmol/L) indicates tissue hypoperfusion — this patient has sepsis with organ dysfunction progressing toward septic shock.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.timeIs: "Time is brain" — approximately 1.9 million neurons are lost per minute during an acute ischemic stroke. Every minute of delay in reperfusion worsens outcomes. Door-to-needle time goal: < 60 minutes.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.tiotro: Tiotropium (Spiriva) 18 mcg daily, fluticasone/salmeterol (Advair) 250/50 BID, albuterol (Ventolin) PRN, lisinopril 10 mg daily.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.toOpti: to optimize cerebral perfusion and reduce ICP risk.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.tropon: Troponin I: 0.8 ng/mL (elevated), BNP: 180 pg/mL, glucose: 185 mg/dL.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.twoLar: Two large-bore IVs (18G or larger). Draw labs: troponin, CK-MB, CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, type & screen.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.warfar: Warfarin 5 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg BID, atorvastatin 80 mg daily, metformin 500 mg BID.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.wbc182: WBC 18,200/μL, lactate 3.8 mmol/L, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (baseline 0.9), glucose 245 mg/dL, procalcitonin 4.2 ng/mL.
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.wifeCa: Wife called 911 reporting that her husband suddenly developed right-sided weakness and "his speech doesn
pages.nursingClinicalScenariosHub.within: within 1 hour of sepsis recognition. Notify surgeon and infectious disease. Broaden from cefazolin to cover both SSI and UTI sources (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin).
